posts.name: Paul Savage
posts.raw:
Brian,

This may be something like the question of who is "better", Kobe Bryant or Lebron James? :slight_smile:

I have trouble coming up with the top 10 producers in France, or even Burgundy, or Bordeaux! :slight_smile:   Maybe if you limit the list to American wines, the question is more approachable?  I have only limited access to the top California wines, living on the East Coast, and have pretty much curtailed my buying due to advancing age, stressed out credit cards, and a full cellar!  What I do have from our West tends to be "old names" like Stag's Leap Cask 23, Mayacamus, Dunn Howell Mtn,, Ridge Monte Bello, Freemark Abbey Boche, Diamond Creek, Montelena, Heitz Martha's, BV George de la Tour, Opus One, and Mondavi Reserve.  But all of this is from the '80s and '90s (and mostly singleton bottles! :frowning:  ).  Your list sounds more "up-to-date"!

posts.name: craig v
posts.raw:
1. Dauvissat - but this will change as I'm "off the list" so to speak with the price hikes
2. Mugnier - also "off this list" too, even though, I adore the wines
3. D Angerville - still buying
4. Charvin - buying for now
5. Rousseau - "off the list" too but, my all time fav producer!
6. Bouchard - mostly white Burgs
7. Chevillon - buying on a modest upswing
8. Haut Bailly - not buying anymore, too expensive
9. Roumier - not buying more, too expensive
10. Le Cadeau Vineyard

Dauvissat is my largest holding with 66 btls.  My list should change a lot in the next 3-5yrs as I start to drink down and reallocate to producers I can still afford. I have 258 produers in my cellar. Hopefully a Barbaresco producer will slip into my top 10 soon.

[quote="craig vanderah"]
Wish I had bought more Giacosa in the past. I'd love for it to be in my top 10 too.  I noticed you have Soldera and Salvioni. Do you own much Biondi Santi? Great list!

[quote="Martin Petersen"]
I'll play

1. Bruno Giacosa
2. Produttori di Barbaresco
3. Montevertine
4. Weingut Keller
5. Elio Altare
6. G. Mascarello
7. B. Mascarello
8. Soldera
9. Salvioni
10. San Giusto a Rentennano

Shows my preferences in style I guess ...

///Martin
[/quote]
[/quote]

Craig, I own one single bottle of Biondi-Santi (1985) but in general I have found these wines to lean and unbalanced for me when tasting them. Poggio di Sotto, Cerbaiona (Molinari) and Monsanto are in my Top 20 so they reflect my next level of preferences. Giacosa - I've been buying from 1996 - 2004 when released and then done some back-filling also. I'm really happy about this as I think neither price nor quality is so attractive any more ....

///Martin

3. Realm

posts.raw:
I totally agree, it's Rousseau at the top of the heap "by a long mile". Fortunately,  I was able to buy a couple mixed cases of 99,01, 05, 06  and 07 before the release prices really started to jump beyond my personal reach. It's really tough to waive goodby to a producer you adore but, no longer can afford. The '99 DRC La Tache is awesome but year in, year out Rousseau speaks to my soul more than any other wine I've ever tasted. The well of fairly priced Rousseau I once had access to, is gone.

I see a few kindred spirits here where there is overlap (including names that are high up on my list but don't quite make the top 10): [quote="salilb"] Donnhoff Bernard Baudry Eric Texier Pierre Gonon Willi Schaefer Franz Hirtzberger Noel Verset JJ Prum Alzinger Tie between Thierry Allemand and Muller-Catoir for 10th [/quote] \
[quote="Tom Maskell"] Not much Italian love on the board. •Bruno Giacosa 5.6% •Produttori del Barbaresco 3.8% •Dönnhoff 2.6% •Fratelli Brovia 2.0% •Giacomo Conterno 1.8% •Giuseppe Rinaldi 1.8% •Poderi Aldo Conterno 1.7% •Domaine Armmand Rousseau 1.5% •Domaine Ponsot 1.4% •Cappellano 1.4% [/quote] Mine looks like this: Produttori di Barbaresco (a lot of the normale/torre bottling) - 4.9% Maximim Grünhaüser (von Schubert) - 3.1% J.J. Prüm - 2.7% Brovia - 2.2% Giacomo Conterno - 2.1% Gouges (may I have a long life) - 2.1% Burlotto - 1.6% Huët - 1.6% Texier (I put my money where my mouth is, David Z ) - 1.5% Dönnhoff - 1.3% This was an interesting exercise. posts.name: John Morris
posts.raw:
A couple of thoughts scanning this:

1. Could someone cut and paste or scrape the lists and run some kind of correlation to see, say, what percentage of William Selyem owners also own X?  Eric L?  (I supposed CellarTracker could compute this over a much larger amount of data.)

2. It's interesting what I don't see much of here here:  Italians other than Barolo/Barbaresco, Spanish, Australian, Austrian.

3. A lot of recurrences of producers with VERY small production (e.g., Allemand).

4. It's amazing how many times Rhys is listed.

posts.name: Michael Davidson
posts.raw:
[quote="John Morris"]
A couple of thoughts scanning this:
3. A lot of recurrences of producers with VERY small production (e.g., Allemand).
[/quote]

For me, this is a combination of two factors. My top producer is <3%, so it doesn't take many bottles to make the list. More importantly, I drink these at a slower rate than other bottles. Allemand is 1.5% of my cellar, but 0.8% of my purchases. I churn through the other bottles much more regularly. It's probably also the case that we all have our favorite mid-range or daily producers, but we're all chasing the same trophies.

Michael

I also see that I need to find a couple of Oregon Pinot producers to buy from, as my list is very Washington heavy.

posts.raw:
It was good to see the lists of many seasoned collectors/drinkers. Over time, this board has introduced me to many European producers and my palate has changed accordingly. Many of the European producers are slowly making their way up my list (but not in top ten yet) as I stop ordering copious amounts of American wine. Ultimately, I'd like to see a few Burgundy producers, a Rhone or two, a Loire, a Rioja, A few Piedmonts, and my tried and true Americans. For now here is my list:

Anthill
Ojai
Rhys
Peay
Rivers Marie
Bodega Muga
Copain
Cameron
Cayuse
Bedrock

This will undoubtedly change, as I am off the list of 4 of the wineries here. Rising producers are Gonon, Allemand, Lopez de Heredia, Paolo Bea, Faiveley, Jadot, Roagna, Vietti.

Curious to see how this will change in 2 years, as I am feverishly (hiccup) drinking through my new world holdings to get more balance.

Fun thread!

posts.name: Bryan McKenney
posts.raw:
Thanks for pointing out the specific issues with the top producers Mike.  I know there were issues with the text and sorting and I had duplicate rows.  And this definitely manifested with producers with the most entries in the thread, skewing the totals like you pointed out.  The data was pretty hard to work with and took me quite a while to get this far.  To get any more accurate I would have had to do some manual consolidation.

But I don't care.  I just know I need to try more Ridge than the one Geyserville I had, and I need to check out Jadot, Trimbach, JJ Prum.  And access to list wines from the US like Rhys will be futile so I might as well focus elsewhere.  And I'm on the right track with Huet and Prod del Barbarresco.  I learned a lot from this thread.  It's like reading years worth of threads in one concise single thread.

I also seem to recall folks wondering at times if this board wasn't too Burgundy-centric - with Rhys, Copain, Rivers-Marie, Saxim & Carlisle all in the top 10, I don't think anyone needs to worry too much about that.
[/quote]

I think the reason for the prevalence of the U.S. producers is the fact that there are a small set of CA producers that a huge % of board members purchase, even if they don't buy broadly in CA.  For example, Rhys is at the top for me, but my cellar is less than 15% domestic overall.  The only lists I'm on are Rhys, Kutch, and Ridge, but those wines build up quickly compared with regular releases.  In Burgundy, Germany, etc., I buy very broadly and have lots of producers where I only have 1-4 bottles total.  I bet my many are in my same situation.

posts.name: Mike Stoneking
posts.raw:
My cellar started very California heavy (98%), and quite focused in Paso Robles. Over the past 5 years I have made an effort to get more diversity in the cellar and have been successful (Cali at 82%). France is now nearly 13% (175 bottles) and Italy is making a recent move at 2.5% (35 bottles). Much more diversity since the last top producers thread.

Bedrock 10.40%
Carlisle 7.40%
Saxum 6.50%
Rhys 5.60%
Villa Creek 4.40%
Linne Calodo 4.10%
Rivers-Marie 4.00%
L'Aventure 3.80%
McPrice Myers 2.70%
Epoch Estate Wines 2.40%

posts.name: Alan C h a n
posts.raw: Interesting exercise, though in my case my results are a bit misleading, both because small overall collection size means a single bargain purchase of a sixer can skew results, and because I buy from a concentrated number of producers in certain regions, but widely in others.  My most represented region is Burgundy, but I buy from a wide range of Burg producers so only one makes my top ten (Bouchard).  There is a small number of CA producers that I buy from but the mailing list minimums push my purchases higher than they might naturally go, and almost all that I buy from are in the top ten: Rhys, Arcadian, Copain, Kutch, Littorai, with Ceritas soon to be knocking on that door.  Also two German producers make my list, Donnhoff and Karthauserhof, with Aldo Conterno on the list because of last year's awesome sale.

But I consider these wines to be great values, and I have acquired them in lieu of other, more expensive, wines that I'd love to drink.  So while you guys may consider me lucky to have a lot of these wines, I am equally jealous of the folks that have listed Dujac, Roumier, etc. in their top ten.

posts.name: BuffaloLou Saad
posts.raw:
Pretty Cali centric (as expected), but starting to get more Italy and Spain.  Doesn't show much here, but more German Rieslings (beyond the Loosen), but most are 1, 2, 3 bottles per producer.

Rosenblum Cellars 4.3% Bottles (131) - Nearly all Zin
Havens Wine Cellars 2.7% Bottles (83) - Bought heavy after closing
Bodegas y Viñedos Maurodos 2.2% Bottles (68) - All San Roman Toro or Toro Prima
Tenuta Monteti 1.8% Bottles (55) - vertical of  Caburnio Toscana
Joseph Phelps 1.7% Bottles (52) - all Insignia or Cabs
Loosen Bros. 1.6% Bottles (48) - all Riesling
Cameron Hughes 1.2% Bottles (36) - mostly Havens "shiners"
Saviez 1.2% Bottles (36) - again, Zin
Torre Quarto 1.2% Bottles (36)

LOL.

The funny thing is, my meager cellar largely overlaps Matt's, yet some of these (my favorite) producers, just do not seem AFWE to me even though they are favorites of the so-called AFWE crowd.  Baudry.  Gonon.  Rougeard (new to me).  Allemand.  Roilette.  Except for perhaps the latter, these are exceptionally flavorful wines.  Roilette clearly has a leaner profile by comparison, though the Griffe du Marquis bottling has extra dimension.

That's of joke, of course!  No one in their right mind would think these (wonderful) wines actually lack flavor.  And we can agree that no one is actually anti-flavor -- people are just anti- "hedonistic gobs of jammy fruit."

That’s of joke, of course! No one in their right mind would think these (wonderful) wines actually lack flavor. And we can agree that no one is actually anti-flavor – people are just anti- “hedonistic gobs of jammy fruit.”

I get what Robert is saying, though. I drink more California wine than anything else, so my idea of “lean” probably differs from someone who drinks a lot of Burgundy. But the producers that Robert highlighted off Matt’s list are big in their own way.

posts.name: N@th@n V@nd€rgrift
posts.raw:
Like Sarah, my list would have been way different in 2014. My access to some wines has shifted dramatically in the intervening years, but at the top they have been that way for a long time. Chave, Allemand and Conterno would have made that list back then, but not now (I still buy Conterno but my allocations are small).

Current cellar:
Domaine Georges Mugneret-Gibourg 7.89%
Domaine Bernard Baudry 3.79%
Eric Texier 3.72%
Clos Rougeard (Foucault) 3.19%
Domaine du Collier & Fratelli Brovia 2.99%
Domaine Ghislaine Barthod 2.71%
Domaine Marquis d'Angerville & Hofgut Falkenstein & G.D. Vajra 2.19%
Hubert Lignier 2.01%
Colombera & Garella 1.91%
Domaine Louis Boillot et Fils 1.88%

Collier, d'Angerville, Falkenstein, Vajra, Colombera & Garella and Louis Boillot are all wines I've been buying in cellaring quantity since 2014 (although I have always had stray bottles of d'Angerville and Vajra).

Mugneret-Gibourg, Baudry, Texier, Clos Rougeard, Barthod, and Lignier are wines I've been buying for a long time.

I a cellar rebalancing exercise, I downloaded consumption by grape variety and then by producer. It's interesting to see what I'm actually drinking versus buying.

Consumption by producer:
2021
Azienda Agricola Caparsa	14
Flavio Roddolo	11
Hofgut Falkenstein	9
Domaine Gramenon	9
Hubert Lignier	8
Domaine Bernard Baudry	8
Tiberio	8
Henri Prudhon & Fils	7
Coudert	7
Azienda Agricola Platinetti Guido	6
Domaine de Marquiliani	6
G.D. Vajra	6
Eric Texier	6
Domaine du Collier	6

2020
Hofgut Falkenstein	25
Henri Prudhon & Fils	20
Flavio Roddolo	19
Domaine Bernard Baudry	14
Domaine Georges Mugneret-Gibourg	13
Azienda Agricola Caparsa	13
Eric Texier	13
Tiberio	12
Coudert	10
Fratelli Brovia	9
Domaine Gramenon	9

2019
Domaine Georges Mugneret-Gibourg	15
Hofgut Falkenstein	15
Vilmart & Cie	14
Eric Texier	12

Ceritas	12

2018
Eric Texier	16

Jean-Philippe Fichet	9
Ferme de la Sansonnière (Mark Angeli)	9
Jacquesson & Fils	7
Rhys	7
Le Piane	7

Also like Sarah, I posted in the Top 5 thread years ago, here is that post for historical accuracy:

[quote=", post:150, topic:114823"]
1    Domaine Bernard Baudry                          12.7%
2    Clos Rougeard (Foucault)                        10.8%
3    Rhys                                             9.7%
4    Domaine Georges Mugneret/Mugneret-Gibourg        9.7%
5    Éric Texier                                      5.2%
6    Ceritas                                          4.8%
7    Coudert                                          4.7%
8    Domaine Louis Boillot et Fils                    3.7%
9    Domaine Ghislaine Barthod / Barthod-Noëllat      3.0%
10    Fratelli Brovia                                  2.6%

This was a good exercise. The amount of Rhys that I own really sneaked up on me. Between that and Ceritas, I'm too heavy on CA pinot noir/chardonnay for my consumption patterns, I think.

I'm happy that Louis Boillot is in there and it should keep rising as I have good access to the wines at good prices (and my 2013s arrive this week). The Barthod bums me out. I just don't have access to the wines anymore at release prices, which are steep enough. I think Rougeard will move down as my allocation has dwindled over the years.

Stalwarts that would have been there a few years back, Conterno and Allemand have dwindled as the prices have soared and I've been a seller instead of a buyer.
[/quote]

So I definitely did some rebalancing of the cellar and my cellar has probably grown overall by 50% since then. The top 10 was 66.9% of my cellar then and it is only 36.7% now. That's great!

Château Branaire-Ducru 2.2%
Ridge 2.1% Bottles
Château Rauzan-Ségla 2.0%
[/quote]

Meyney is a really interesting one to have so much of. I probably should have bought more of this over the years. Several years ago, a good friend brought a bottle of 1982 to a tasting and I was really amazed at how well it had aged over the years. What recent vintages have you particularly liked?
[/quote]

Meyney is definitely a great value play - probably my favorite BDX at that price point.

The 2014 is serious juice and reminded me of Calon Segur, but with a bit more acid and a bit less brawn.  I liked the 2016 even more when it first arrived -- the complete package -- but I haven't tried it since.  Both seem built to last like the "Meyneys of old".  2012 is a classic claret with a elegant if lighter profile; not "serious" but very enjoyable.  The 2009 is also quite nice in a richer vein, but still very taut and needed more time as of 2 years ago.

The only time I've tried a 2015 it was very flat and maybe slightly corked, so I can't comment!  And I haven't had the 2010.

Meyney is definitely a great value play - probably my favorite BDX at that price point.

The 2014 is serious juice and reminded me of Calon Segur, but with a bit more acid and a bit less brawn. I liked the 2016 even more when it first arrived – the complete package – but I haven’t tried it since. Both seem built to last like the “Meyneys of old”. 2012 is a classic claret with a elegant if lighter profile; not “serious” but very enjoyable. The 2009 is also quite nice in a richer vein, but still very taut and needed more time as of 2 years ago.

The only time I’ve tried a 2015 it was very flat and maybe slightly corked, so I can’t comment! And I haven’t had the 2010.

Of course that is “lifetime” consumption. So far, in 2021 Rhys is in first. Gee, for 2020 and 2019 too. Gotta go to 2018 to find a different domaine holding spot number one. You might think it is a Burgundy or even German winery, but no - it is Vajra, which finished #2 in 2019. Fact is, I drink a lot of different Burgundy producers. Now if I sort by region, Burgundy finishes in first place for every year since I started using CT (i.e., since 2007).

Let's see what has changed in 8 years . . .

Saxum

SQN
Aubert
Cayuse
Rivers Marie
Black Sears
Outpost
Benovia
Pax (tie with Benovia)

Not much changed in style, but producers leap frog each other. Changes are interesting but not unexpected. Hospices obviously drops because there is no more. SQN shoots up because I just got on the list in 2012 or 2013. Black Sears shoots up because we (my wife more than me) just discovered it in 2012. Same with Benovia (also "her" winery). Cayuse is going up since I finally got a chance to buy The Frog and The Pig, so I buy more. OUtpost is dropping because they sold to a conglomerate. Pax is dropping because he went over to the dark side (just kidding). My guess is that Scarecrow is probably in the top 10 but I have not entered all of the boxes in CT and MacDonald is getting close to breaking into the top 10.

Not much changed in style, but producers leap frog each other. Changes are interesting but not unexpected. Hospices obviously drops because there is no more. SQN shoots up because I just got on the list in 2012 or 2013. Black Sears shoots up because we (my wife more than me) just discovered it in 2012. Same with Benovia (also “her” winery). Cayuse is going up since I finally got a chance to buy The Frog and The Pig, so I buy more. OUtpost is dropping because they sold to a conglomerate. Pax is dropping because he went over to the dark side (just kidding). My guess is that Scarecrow is probably in the top 10 but I have not entered all of the boxes in CT and MacDonald is getting close to breaking into the top 10.

Red Burgundy is actually my largest cellar holding, at about 14% of my bottles, but I buy a little more broadly there than in other regions, so no domaine makes the top 10.  The largest Red Burg holding is Simon Bize, which ranks 19th overall.  My average bottle age now is 10 years, which would actually be a little alarming given the size of my cellar, but that's driven up by a number of old Nebbioli.
[/quote]

My list looks a bit similar. I have widely varied holdings, but, because I started buying much later than many here, with small allocations and small purchases of wines that appreciated and became scarce, numbers for those wines are low. But Burgs are 17% of my total, though the only  producers even cracking my top 30 are Jouan and Jadot. My average vintage is 2008, and fully 78.5% of my wine dates from 2010 or more recent (mostly, to me, not ready to drink at all).

Top Ten (only 13% of cellar)
Bedrock
Ridge
Musar
Cameron
Chartogne-Taillet
Dirty and Rowdy
JJ Prum
Levet
Calluna
Peay

posts.name: David Baum
posts.raw:
Dont know that Ive done this before but I kinda knew

1. Rivers-Marie - because I love everything they produce. No end to buying in sight
2. Scherrer - because I became infatuated with his zins aged after a tasting there several years ago and have been buying the futures but Im good now
3. Dehlinger - one of my early heavy buying producers, have scaled back and will enjoy drinking down. Will continue annual rose buy but can get others at retail now
4. Kanzler - my ex-GF favorite but she's gone and will be drinking over the years in her memory #RIPIslandMelodee
5. Andremily - been buying for a few years but they need so much time they just pile up for now
6. Bedrock - kinda over it, will buy selectively
7. Peay -used to buy a lot but have scaled back last few years. Really love them and their wines. Need to scale back others and buy more from them
8. Third Twin-just piling up, not sure when I'll ever drink em
9. Altesino- loaded up on 2010's when I was there in 2015 and then you know had to load up on 2015's because I was there in 2015 and saw the grapes on the vines
10. MacDonald - will continue growing albeit slowly for now. Have been on list long enough that they are starting to reach drinking windows so will drink 1 or 2 a year

Those 10 make up about 40% of the cellar. The other 60% is 117 producers

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.raw:
Top 10 by number of bottles (which represent about a quarter of my cellar)

Bruno Giacosa
Domaine Robert Arnoux / Arnoux-Lachaux
Domaine Fourrier
Domaine Robert Groffier
Littorai
Willi Schaefer
Alain Hudelot-Noellat
Joh. Jos. Prüm
G.D. Vajra
Bartolo Mascarello/Hofgut Falkenstein (tie)

Compared to what I actually drank in the last couple of years (by region/type):
German Riesling - 26%
Red Burg - 14%
Piedmont Nebbiolo - 13%
Cal Cab/Bordeaux Blends - 9%
Champagne - 7%
White Burg - 6.5%
Bordeaux - 5%
N. Rhone - 4%

I thought it was more interesting to look at it this way since what I drink tends to be extremely varied. My top two though are Willi Schaefer and JJ Prüm - and far more than any others.

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
Interesting to see, especially the development over time.

My top 10 (out of 280):

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Henrik Möbitz
5. Weiser-Künstler
6. Stein
7 Daniel Bouland
8. Ca' del Baio
9. La Ca'Növa
10. Baudry/Dettori

Only a few bottles apart from 10 and 20 with more Italian (Barolo mainly) producers.

A bit surprised to see how may Germany entries in top 10, knew E&M as well as Möbitz would be quite high up. 6-9 are wines more recently purchased, the two Barbaresco producers plenty of 2016 bottles (birth year).

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.raw:
[quote=", post:63, topic:74370"]
comte-liger belair, de montille, mugnier, leroy, pernot, angerville, boisson vadot, dauvissat, chandon de briailles,de vogue.  However, there isn't that much separation between the top 10 and the 5 or six producers.
[/quote]

Wow.  It’s crazy for me to look at this now. Sold all the Liger-Belair because of the oak. Drank the Leroy. Stopped buying white burgundy and de vogue. Now it’s:

Keller
Julian Haart
Schloss Lieser
Fourrier
Prum
Rousseau
Willi Schaefer
Lauer
Jean Marc Bouley
Grunhaus

the bulk is Percarlo, as I drink the cases of CC too quickly. I do manage to save some Le Baroncole, but not enough; love opening for friends!
[/quote]

Maybe it's the CCR (?) that I'm remembering, but isn't the CC about $65?  I've a couple Percarlo squirreled-away, but have never actually tried a wine from this producer --- something I need to change.

2021 update:
1. Domaine Ghislaine Barthod
2. Domaine Michel Lafarge
3. Bartolo Mascarello
4. Cappellano
5. Giuseppe Rinaldi
6. Chartogne-Taillet
7. Domaine Fourrier
8. Prevost
9. Domaine Marquis d'Angerville
10. Clos Rougeard

Bachelet is now #18 - seems about right, they are never ready, but love them when they finally do get ready.
Gibourg is now #14 - still have a lot
Schaefer is probably #100 or so - just don't drink as much German riesling as I once did
Houillon is #20 - can't buy them anymore, but luckily I have quite a lot
Baudry is #21 - not sure what happened here, must fix that
Ganevat is around #50 - I loved the orig. J'en Veux when it was still available and still dig Marguerite and Pere.

-mark

Fun to see my wine buying and drinking patterns.  Obviously I like Chardonnay and Riesling!  But looking at it by appellation shows a different pattern.  Consumption is just within the last 3 years when I started using Cellartracker.  And I see as a region, I have a lot of Bordeaux but with multiple producers and I don't tend to drink that often.  I will have to fix that.

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
[quote="Mikael_OB, post:464, topic:74370, full:true"]
Interesting to see, especially the development over time.

My top 10 (out of 280):

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Henrik Möbitz
5. Weiser-Künstler
6. Stein
7 Daniel Bouland
8. Ca' del Baio
9. La Ca'Növa
10. Baudry/Dettori

Only a few bottles apart from 10 and 20 with more Italian (Barolo mainly) producers.

A bit surprised to see how may Germany entries in top 10, knew E&M as well as Möbitz would be quite high up. 6-9 are wines more recently purchased, the two Barbaresco producers plenty of 2016 bottles (birth year).
[/quote]

One of the main change is that white wine producers have climbed up a few spots. Intentional change, as a) didn't have enough, to high red wine ratio in general b) my daughters birth year is 2020 and that give me even more motivation to fill up on white wines (many red have not yet been released yet like Barolo/Barbaresco). 

Sandri and Burlotto has worked their way to the top 10 list as well, and that's related to "b" to some extent as I have bought quite a few of the 2020 LNs, but also 2016s Sandri Barolos (my son's birth year). For me the Burlotto is more near term consumption (perhaps medium and up to around 10 years I can imagine), while the Sandri LN have the stuffing for aging - I have found them to many times be better/more real with soul then many Barolos. 

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Stein
5. Weiser-Künstler
6. Henrik Möbitz 
7. Elio Sandri
8. Comm. G.B. Burlotto
9. Hofgut Falkenstein 
10. Weingut Keller

A few bottles bottles behind 10th are the wines that got pushed out of the list.
posts.cooked:
Mikael_OB:

Interesting to see, especially the development over time.

My top 10 (out of 280):

  1. Ronchi di Cialla
  2. Milos
  3. Enderle & Moll
  4. Henrik Möbitz
  5. Weiser-Künstler
  6. Stein
    7 Daniel Bouland
  7. Ca’ del Baio
  8. La Ca’Növa
  9. Baudry/Dettori

Only a few bottles apart from 10 and 20 with more Italian (Barolo mainly) producers.

A bit surprised to see how may Germany entries in top 10, knew E&M as well as Möbitz would be quite high up. 6-9 are wines more recently purchased, the two Barbaresco producers plenty of 2016 bottles (birth year).

One of the main change is that white wine producers have climbed up a few spots. Intentional change, as a) didn’t have enough, to high red wine ratio in general b) my daughters birth year is 2020 and that give me even more motivation to fill up on white wines (many red have not yet been released yet like Barolo/Barbaresco).

Sandri and Burlotto has worked their way to the top 10 list as well, and that’s related to “b” to some extent as I have bought quite a few of the 2020 LNs, but also 2016s Sandri Barolos (my son’s birth year). For me the Burlotto is more near term consumption (perhaps medium and up to around 10 years I can imagine), while the Sandri LN have the stuffing for aging - I have found them to many times be better/more real with soul then many Barolos.

  1. Ronchi di Cialla
  2. Milos
  3. Enderle & Moll
  4. Stein
  5. Weiser-Künstler
  6. Henrik Möbitz
  7. Elio Sandri
  8. Comm. G.B. Burlotto
  9. Hofgut Falkenstein
  10. Weingut Keller

A few bottles bottles behind 10th are the wines that got pushed out of the list.

posts.name: Jim Hanlon
posts.raw:
[quote="Jim_Hanlon, post:388, topic:74370, full:true"]
1. Jacques Puffeney 2.6%
2. Produttori del Barbaresco 2.2%
3. JJ Prum 2.2%
3. Donnhoff 2.1%
4. Gonon 2.0%
5. Mount Eden 1.9%
6. Willi Schaefer 1.9%
7. Huet 1.8%
8. Bedrock 1.5%
9. Max Ferd. Richter 1.5%
10. Baudry 1.5%

Red Burgundy is actually my largest cellar holding, at about 14% of my bottles, but I buy a little more broadly there than in other regions, so no domaine makes the top 10.  The largest Red Burg holding is Simon Bize, which ranks 19th overall.  My average bottle age now is 10 years, which would actually be a little alarming given the size of my cellar, but that's driven up by a number of old Nebbioli.
[/quote]

Two and half years later, my list is pretty consistent:

1.  J.J. Prum
2.  Jacques Puffeney
3.  Produttori del Barbaresco
4.  Falkenstein
5.  Gonon
6.  Donnhoff
7.  Willi Schaefer
8.  Bedrock
9.  Mount Eden
10.  Max Ferd. Richter

These 10 are about 17.5% of my cellar.  Red Burgundy is still my largest holding, even though not reflected in my top 10 domaines.  Top Burg domaines are Bize (19), Chandon de Briailles (28), Pavelot (30), Camus-Bruchon (35), and Guillemot (37).  I'm obviously value-oriented in Burgundy.

Bonus top individual wines:

1.  Gonon St. Joe (16 vintages)
2.  Mount Eden Pinot Noir (25 vintages)
3.  Clos de la Roilette Fleurie Cuvee Tardive (12 vintages)

  9. Bedrock 1.5%
  10. Max Ferd. Richter 1.5%
  11. Baudry 1.5%

Red Burgundy is actually my largest cellar holding, at about 14% of my bottles, but I buy a little more broadly there than in other regions, so no domaine makes the top 10. The largest Red Burg holding is Simon Bize, which ranks 19th overall. My average bottle age now is 10 years, which would actually be a little alarming given the size of my cellar, but that’s driven up by a number of old Nebbioli.

Two and half years later, my list is pretty consistent:

  1. J.J. Prum
  2. Jacques Puffeney
  3. Produttori del Barbaresco
  4. Falkenstein
  5. Gonon
  6. Donnhoff
  7. Willi Schaefer
  8. Bedrock
  9. Mount Eden
  10. Max Ferd. Richter

These 10 are about 17.5% of my cellar. Red Burgundy is still my largest holding, even though not reflected in my top 10 domaines. Top Burg domaines are Bize (19), Chandon de Briailles (28), Pavelot (30), Camus-Bruchon (35), and Guillemot (37). I’m obviously value-oriented in Burgundy.

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.raw:
JMT Bouley, Duroche, G. Rinaldi, Cappellano, Jamet, Burlotto, Faiveley, Hudelot-Noellat, Chevillon, VCC.

I only started buying a few years ago and 7 of the 10 producers have doubled in price since I started buying them and I no longer buy some of them.

Methodology: my cellar has some chunky single vintage holdings. I looked at my largest 30 overall producers and then adjusted by stripping out the two largest vintages I own of those producers - and then re-ranked the remaining. I.E., these are producers where I have some quantity beyond just 2 vintages.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.raw:
Several differences between now and a few years ago.  I'm quite sure that Bedrock, Richter, Prüm, Schaefer, and Halcón would not have been on this list five years ago.

I have much more red wine than white, but I have spread my red purchases around a lot more than my white purchases, which explains why 4 of my top 10 are white wine producers.  

Also, with my cellar as diverse as it is, these top 10 range only from 2.3% of the total, down to 0.9%.

Finally, it's curious that my top 3 holdings by region are Bordeaux, Burgundy, and Northern Rhône, but there are no producers from any of these regions on my list of top 10 producers.  Again, that is because I spread those purchases around a lot over the years, whereas (for example) the vast majority of my zin purchases used to be Ridge and Turley and are now Ridge and Bedrock.  My top Bordeaux producer is Cantemerle, way down at number 20 overall, with Chevillon (15) and Clusel-Roch (23) taking the Burg and NR top spots.

Bedrock
Max. Ferd. Richter
Beaucastel
Ridge
Huet
JJ Prüm
Fèlsina
Willi Schaefer
Lisini
Halcón

I've also been tracking consumption since Sept. '17 in CT, and it's interesting that only 2 of these producers (Fèlsina and Bedrock) are in my top 10 of consumed bottles in that relatively small snapshot of drinking.  Daily drinkers obviously tend to cycle through the buy-store-drink process faster than many of the producers on my top 10 by inventory, and producers of longer-aging wines are going to have a lag where consumption reflects mostly who I was buying 20 years ago and inventory reflects those I've bought more recently, although the top 10 includes several I have bought continuously since getting into wine (Beaucastel, Ridge, Fèlsina, Lisini).

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
Hi Ofer, 

It’s a good question, and a combination of a few different things. 

The first is that the previous 7-10 (Daniel Bouland, Ca’ del Baio, La Ca’Növa, Baudry/Dettori) are all a few bottles short of the 10th spot. That’s not due to consumption, just more of other wines coming in. 

Secondly I had more focus/priority on white wines given the red/white ratio and my daughter birth vintage being 2020. 

Thirdly, loaded up quite a bit on La Ca’Növa (and to some extent Ca’ del Baio) with 2016 being my son’s birth year. Haven’t really bought much since, found the 2017s to reflect the heat of the vintage and the Barbarescos’ felt quite tired when I tried them. I have since seen positive reviews on CT that sounds like completely different wines than I had. But I don’t rule out that they made one of those spectacular transformations only Nebbiolo can pull off…

While still good value for money, they are not the bargain the used to be before 2019 was released. For the same reason I got quite a bit of white 2020 Riesling. I’m hoping to find some interesting Barbaresco wines… Didn’t hear much yet from the tastings that took place in Langhe recently for the 2020 Barbarescos and 2019 Barolos but I guess we will start reading more soon.
posts.cooked:

Secondly I had more focus/priority on white wines given the red/white ratio and my daughter birth vintage being 2020.

Thirdly, loaded up quite a bit on La Ca’Növa (and to some extent Ca’ del Baio) with 2016 being my son’s birth year. I’m hoping to find some interesting Barbaresco wines… Didn’t hear much yet from the tastings that took place in Langhe recently for the 2020 Barbarescos and 2019 Barolos but I guess we will start reading more soon.

posts.cooked: Whites or reds(RdC)?

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
Both - of the reds I have more Schippettino than Refosco dal Peduncolo and Pignolo. As for the whites, mainly the CiallaBianco and Ribolla Gialla along with minor quantities of the sweet wines (both Verduzzo and Picolit). 

For me it’s probably one of, if not the greatest Italian wineries still kind of under the radar. I can’t think of a winery making higher quality wines with such a wide range of varieties and styles of wines. 

The CiallaBianco can be spectacular with age, had the 2005 about 3 years ago and I wrote down the following:

“Profound. Impressive complexity, elegance and structure (mouthfeel that captivates). Balanced, lively and energetic, even more so on the fourth night.

At a certain moment, having closed my eyes, it reminded of the feel of a very elegant aged nebbiolo wine, maybe because of the mouth feel together with some of the flavors I love in an aged and good Barolo/Barbaresco.”
posts.cooked:

For me it’s probably one of, if not the greatest Italian wineries still kind of under the radar. I can’t think of a winery making higher quality wines with such a wide range of varieties and styles of wines.

The CiallaBianco can be spectacular with age, had the 2005 about 3 years ago and I wrote down the following:

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.raw:
When using CT stats on value, it appears to pull from its auction/Wine Market Journal Data.

For example, the last bottle I drank was a 2007 Còlpetrone SdM.  On the page for that wine, it shows an "community average value" of $25.97, and an auction/WMJ value of $14.93.  And when I ask CT for a list of recently consumed bottles, it is the $14.93 that shows up in the "value" field for that wine.

That can also create problems for very rare bottles, like the Lisini Profillossero I had recently, where it shows a value of zero because, I assume, WMJ has no auction data.

Bouchard Père et Fils 1.5% (Corton, who knew...?)
Joseph Drouhin 1.4% (Jeez, the quality here...)

Here is how I *wish* it stacked up, knowing what I know now...
More Fourrier, Niellon and PYCM whites and a more "ticket" wines from 15'. 

Going forward?  More backfilling, fewer "daily drinkers". 
So, so many good glasses from Germany. Keep them coming. White Burgs and good Chards from Oregon? Oh yes. 
No more Marechale, sorry Frederic. And, though happy with those hedonistic le Moines, I'm probably done. 

Full speed ahead on Drouhin, Gelin and up-and-comers (Audoin, Lafouge, Corton and Marsannay/Fixin/Beaune generally) plus some ticket bottles and "curious George" samples.
posts.cooked:
Ok so. I am at 70% French, 68% Burgundy and though I blush to say it, cellar bottle #s above 2000.
Even so CT says:

Joh. Jos. Prüm 5.6% (A blast in the glass, every single, time- and so many other great producers to sip)
Louis Jadot 4.0%
Lucien Le Moine 3.3%
Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier 3.1% (99.9% Marechale)
Walter Scott 3.0% (Be told)
Domaine Henri Boillot 1.9%
Domaine Charles Audoin 1.9%
Domaine Pierre Gelin 1.5% (Be told, again…)
Bouchard Père et Fils 1.5% (Corton, who knew…?)
Joseph Drouhin 1.4% (Jeez, the quality here…)

Here is how I wish it stacked up, knowing what I know now…
More Fourrier, Niellon and PYCM whites and a more “ticket” wines from 15’.

Going forward? More backfilling, fewer “daily drinkers”.
So, so many good glasses from Germany. Keep them coming. White Burgs and good Chards from Oregon? Oh yes.
No more Marechale, sorry Frederic. And, though happy with those hedonistic le Moines, I’m probably done.

Full speed ahead on Drouhin, Gelin and up-and-comers (Audoin, Lafouge, Corton and Marsannay/Fixin/Beaune generally) plus some ticket bottles and “curious George” samples.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.raw:
J. J. Prum 5.8%. - Surprised: went a little nuts on the 2019s. WS and GH kabs and spats
J.M. Fourrier 4.9% - Not surprised: went a lot nuts for the Gevrey vv, aux Moines and CSJ
Jadot 4.5% - Who knew release tastings were a risk factor for buying a lot of wine?! :wow: Ursules rules. 
Jos. Drouhin 3% -  Big fan.
Le Moine 2.9%. - I get a lot of pleasure here, even if the wines are not as exalted as some domaine wines
Henri Boillot, Audoin 1.9% - Marsannay people. Be told
Trapet 1.7% - Love the 15’ Chambertin
M. Mallard, Chevillon 1.5% - Chevillon’s Cailles is a winner

Coming up from behind lately:
Berthaut Gerbet
Champagne, champagne, champagne

Wish I had more:
Hudelot-Noellat
Cecile Tremblay

Three years later...and consumption has changed a little...mostly dropping Keller because they're getting harder & harder to find at affordable prices here in the US.  Bartlett Estate is a wine I open on every Birthday now...and considering that he's not making anymore wine, I expect that number will only continue to shrink over the years.  Always a fun experiment.

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.raw:
Current top 12 (greater than 50 bottles of each)

1. Wendouree
2. Donnhoff
3. Rousseau
4. Mugnier
5. Chave
6. Raveneau
7. Rayas
8. Hudelot Noellat
9. Giacosa
10. Dujac
11. Roumier
12. Magdelaine

The big changes since 2013 are the decline of the legacy Aussies (Penfolds, Rockford, Yarra Yering and Yeringburg). I had a shit ton of Wendouree back then , now merely a lot. 

The biggest movers are Rayas and Magdelaine.  Ten years ago I had two bottles of Rayas and zero Magdelaine.  I am hoping Magdelaine and Rayas will continue to grow and move up.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.raw:
:scratch:

I would say that trying to extract any data in WB threads as indicating some sort of truth about how people feel about J.J. Prum isn’t possible.  I can honestly say I’ve never met a German Mosel wine geek who likes and buys Kabinett, Spatlese, and Auslese wines and who doesn’t revere J.J. Prum.  

Anecdotally, for a long time I had more Prum in the cellar than any other German winemaker.  And I’d have a lot more today if I hadn’t opened so many over the last 25 years.
posts.cooked:

Anecdotally, for a long time I had more Prum in the cellar than any other German winemaker. And I’d have a lot more today if I hadn’t opened so many over the last 25 years.

Scott,

Of my 180 bottles of Produttori slightly more than half is diveded between Rabaja and Asili. Then comes Pora, Montefico, Montestefano and Paje with around 15 bottles each and then and few Moccagatta. 
Vintages range from 1967 to 2015 but especially 89, 99, 01, 04, 05, 08, 11 and 13 from which I hold more than 12 bottles each vintage. I don’t buy these anymore but drink them from time to time when in Piemonte.
Btw. my father used to buy the “normale” in every vintage as their “house wine”. We think desrly of him every time ee open a bottle, most recently 06 and 08. What great values they were.

Nice list of producers you have in your cellar 😃

posts.name: Marco Fimiani
posts.cooked:
My list (for now):

  • Altesino (3.8%);
  • Mastroberardino (3.8%);
  • Leoville Poyferre (3.7);
  • Il Poggione (3.5%);
  • Paolo Scavino (3.0%);
  • Elderton (2.9%);
  • G.D. Vajra (2.6%);
  • Chateau Canon (2.6%);
  • La Rogerie (1.9%);
  • Chateau Barde Haut (1.9%).

I can foresee a few changes over the next year or two with Barde Haut dropping off (the price increases are seemingly making less sense for them) and other wineries (in addition to Altos Las Hormigas) like Tenuta Argentiera, Castello dei Rampolla and Fontodi (maybe also Standish) making their way up. G.D. Vajra will also surely make more of a climb.

Elderton is a bit of an odd ball in the list but I buy a case in every strong vintage given its one of the most profound wine drinking memories I have shared with my father.

posts.name: J0zef P1nxten
posts.raw:
I have only had it with 2 bottles of 2013 Riservas (don't recall the cru), but a friend of mine who bought from the same batch also had issues. I have not had the same issues with younger bottles (2014 and 2015), and don't have older ones. The bottles were bought from the Belgian importer. When I checked cellartracker, it didn't seem like a common problem.

I have read a couple of times that these bottles don't hold as long as they did historically, but that's just hearsay, and I just wanted to hear from someone with more experience than I have. 

So I'd say my experience is anecdotal at best.

Edit: some references to oxidised Produtorri 2013s here so not that uncommon @Joseph_Grassa ?

TN: Produttori del Barbaresco vertical 2016-1970 WINE TALK
2016 Produttori del Barbaresco Barbaresco - Italy, Piedmont, Langhe, Barbaresco (24.10.2020) This bottle is from Lot 19.058, ie. the wine is bottled in late February 2019, after some two-plus years of aging in large botti casks. 14,5% alcohol. Decanted for 1½ hours. Translucent, luminous ruby red color. Somewhat restrained yet very attractive and subtly sweetish nose with aromas of licorice, some strawberry, a little bit of ripe black cherry, light candied notes of raspberry marmalade and a …
UPDATE: Auction purchase - wine beyond damaged WINE TALK
I’ve had in the last month a Congo Ravera 2006 and Produttori Barbaresco 2013 come out similarly brown and stinky. Clearly oxidised and I suspect some heat damage thrown in for good measure. Bad luck.
Tried my first Produttori Del Barbaresco… WINE TALK
Not the regular, but it appears that all of my 2013 Riservas are damaged. I tried a Pora that was clearly affected, so then I tried an Ovello with the same results. Another bottle of Ovello I got through the second source was also very advanced. Since then a Montestefano and an Asilli both badly advanced. Two different retailers in two different states with two different distribution channels, so I can only surmise that something happened on the way to the US from Italy.

I am very curious about this. This seems to be the thinking in general. I have loved the older PdB that I have had and I am very fond of 1967 Nebbiolo in general. I wasn't even born then, so I have no idea how the 67s tasted when they were young, but I do find plenty of similarities between 2016 and 1967, much more so than say 71 or 78. My hopes are that the 2016 has similar longevity, but only time will tell!

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.raw:
Not that it matters, but since there are so many burgheads here, I thought I should throw down the Wine with Flavor gauntlet. I went to 11 because I wanted to prove that my cellar has balance. This list is approximate because I have been very negligent for the past few years. My guess is that SQN and Saxum are up, Aubert and Outpost are down, Hospices de Beaune is down, and the rest are probably up in number of bottles but level in % of cellar. Average vintage is up because I have not added any wine to CT for a few years - waiting to complete the cellar reorganziation.

* ### **[Saxum](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Saxum)** 11.6%

Bottles (235) / Average vintage 2012.8

* ### **[Carlisle](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Carlisle)** 10.0%

Bottles (202) / Average vintage 2012.1

* ### **[Sine Qua Non](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Sine+Qua+Non)** 5.3%

Bottles (108) / Average vintage 2014.0

* ### **[Aubert](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Aubert)** 3.9%

Bottles (79) / Average vintage 2010.9

* ### **[Cayuse](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Cayuse)** 3.5%

Bottles (71) / Average vintage 2011.8

* ### **[Rivers-Marie](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Rivers%2DMarie)** 3.4%

Bottles (69) / Average vintage 2012.4

* ### **[Black Sears](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Black+Sears)** 3.2%

Bottles (65) /  Average vintage 2014.5

* ### **[Outpost](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Outpost)** 2.6%

Bottles (53) / Average vintage 2010.7

* ### **[Benovia](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Benovia)** 2.2%

Bottles (44) / Average vintage 2015.1

* ### **[Pax](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Pax)** 2.2%

Bottles (44) / Average vintage 2005.8

* ### **[Hospices de Beaune](https://www.cellartracker.com/list.asp?table=List&Producer=Hospices+de+Beaune)** 1.8%

Bottles (37) / Average vintage 2004.8

In no order but grouped by region.
Ch. Cantemerle
Ch. Gloria
Ch. La Lagune
G D Vajra
Produttori del Barbaresco (mostly the flagship bottling but a few of the crus)

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.raw:
Last month I sold another chunk to Chambers St (lots of daily drinkers, plus a good quantity of Juge, Vatan, Texier, Huet, and other Rhone, Germany, and Loire; they started putting them on sale last week).  My current cellar composition in descending order:

Louis Roederer
Alexandre Filaine
Taittinger
Juge
Dom Perignon
Egly
Clape
Vatan
EWCY!
Krug, Donnhoff, Huet tied for 10th

Prior to the beginning of my cellar purge in 2022, I owned a half-dozen producers in 200-500  bottle quantity.  After the Chambers St. sale, Roederer is the only producer >100.

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.raw:
[quote="Steve_McL, post:768, topic:74370, full:true"]
Wow! Now THAT is a diversified list! Love Cappellano and Hudelot-Noellat
[/quote]

Cappellano has been solid for me as the wines age faster than I generally expect. So less waiting on those. 

I asked ChatGPT for the common thread, and this is what I got:
*The wines from these top producers share a commitment to balancing ripeness, structure, and tannins. This balance ensures their wines are both expressive of their terroir and capable of aging gracefully. Modern innovations have allowed these producers to refine their tannins and enhance their wines' approachability without sacrificing the complexity and depth that come from traditional methods. This harmonious integration of old and new techniques results in wines that are vibrant, structured, and full of character.*

posts.name: G av_1n 40r es1
posts.raw:
Jamet
Donnhoff
Tempier
Walter Scott
Jean-Louis Dutraive (Domaine de la Grand'Cour)
Goodfellow
No Girls
Scholium Project
Gonon
Cotat

This feels mostly right. Have drunk down some mainstays that no longer please (Marguet for example probably would have been top 3). Also changed up buying strategy.

Perdictions forwards. Jamet probably stays level. Donnhoff gains as I backfill vertical. Tempier depends on backfilling (guessing down) as the 2000 onwards change is questionable to me. WS is such a great value. Grand'Cour probably gets overtaken given prices are up. Same with Gonon and maybe Cotat, though Cotat will hang for longer. Was surprised on Scholium, No girls, and Goodfellow. First two probably get overtaken for no particular reason. Goodfellow tbd - need to get hands on some back vintages. What comes up? Still drinking more regionally than producer forwards so can't really tell.

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
[quote="Mikael_OB, post:526, topic:74370, full:true"]
[quote="Mikael_OB, post:464, topic:74370, full:true"]
Interesting to see, especially the development over time.

My top 10 (out of 280):

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Henrik Möbitz
5. Weiser-Künstler
6. Stein
7 Daniel Bouland
8. Ca' del Baio
9. La Ca'Növa
10. Baudry/Dettori

Only a few bottles apart from 10 and 20 with more Italian (Barolo mainly) producers.

A bit surprised to see how may Germany entries in top 10, knew E&M as well as Möbitz would be quite high up. 6-9 are wines more recently purchased, the two Barbaresco producers plenty of 2016 bottles (birth year).
[/quote]

One of the main change is that white wine producers have climbed up a few spots. Intentional change, as a) didn't have enough, to high red wine ratio in general b) my daughters birth year is 2020 and that give me even more motivation to fill up on white wines (many red have not yet been released yet like Barolo/Barbaresco). 

Sandri and Burlotto has worked their way to the top 10 list as well, and that's related to "b" to some extent as I have bought quite a few of the 2020 LNs, but also 2016s Sandri Barolos (my son's birth year). For me the Burlotto is more near term consumption (perhaps medium and up to around 10 years I can imagine), while the Sandri LN have the stuffing for aging - I have found them to many times be better/more real with soul then many Barolos. 

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Stein
5. Weiser-Künstler
6. Henrik Möbitz 
7. Elio Sandri
8. Comm. G.B. Burlotto
9. Hofgut Falkenstein 
10. Weingut Keller

A few bottles bottles behind 10th are the wines that got pushed out of the list.
[/quote]

About a year and a half later and there are some movement, mainly driven by focusing on adding white wines, and working on the cellar reduction plan has forced me to be even more focused in purchases. 

The main changes are that Falkenstein, Stein and Keller has kept moving on up. 
Ca Nova was just outside top 10 last time, a few bottles of 2020 (birth year wines) added and they're back in. 

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Falkenstein
5. Stein
6. Keller
7. Weiser-Künstler
8. La Ca' Növa
9. Burlotto
10.  Henrik Möbitz / Sandri

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.raw:
As you probably read, Mosel Fine Wines wrote, regarding Hofgut Falkenstien, that 2023 "is possibly the finest vintage here since the magical 2016 vintage." And that's saying something. They also felt that 2014 and 2015 were tops. But between 2016 and 2023, the wines seemed to have reached another level. 

I would say that "the contradictory nature of the wines" comes from the low yields and their vinification methods.

posts.name: Steve Costigan
posts.raw: Sarah-I’m curious about your thoughts on Daniel Rion. Seeing that alongside the others on your list made me think of “which one is not like the other?”. While a few bottles of Rion have found their way into my cellar I wouldn’t have thought they were on the same level as most of the others on your list. I’m wondering if I should revaluate that which is why I’m curious about knowing more. Cheers.
posts.cooked: Sarah-I’m curious about your thoughts on Daniel Rion. Seeing that alongside the others on your list made me think of “which one is not like the other?”. While a few bottles of Rion have found their way into my cellar I wouldn’t have thought they were on the same level as most of the others on your list. I’m wondering if I should revaluate that which is why I’m curious about knowing more. Cheers.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.raw:
[quote="Steve_Costigan, post:787, topic:74370, full:true"]
Sarah-I’m curious about your thoughts on Daniel Rion. Seeing that alongside the others on your list made me think of “which one is not like the other?”. While a few bottles of Rion have found their way into my cellar I wouldn’t have thought they were on the same level as most of the others on your list. I’m wondering if I should revaluate that which is why I’m curious about knowing more. Cheers.
[/quote]

I don't know - Vallana and Laible aren't winning any label whore contests, I'd wager. ;-)

But seriously, first of all I like Daniel Rion, and some of the younger Rion labels as well.  They are not "high level" and no one is going to brag about them, but I find them to be honest, well made, interesting, expressive of place, without excessive winemaking signature, balanced, food friendly, and they age nicely.  Perhaps on the rustic side, but I'd take that over polished most days of the week. Almost as importantly, I can get them and afford them.

My current cellar composition is largely driven by the fact that neither my husband nor I have any interest in one or two bottles of a wine.  If I can't have a case, or at least a 6 pack, we mostly don't want it.  Of course there are splurge exceptions and some very rare bottles that I will still buy in ones or twos, but mostly our interest is in wines we can buy in quantity, that go with food and will age well.  I can afford cases of most of the other producers on my list, or I could at one time (I'm not buying Giacosa anymore, but I bought enough of it back in the day that it still remains a large holding.  Same with Ulysse Collin)  Mugnier and Roumier used to be on my Top 10 list, but as I have drunk those holdings down and haven't replaced them due to price, not to mention bought a lot of Riesling (thanks, Dear Husband) they have dropped off.  

I'm sure there are other Burgundy producers that fall into the same bracket as Rion.  Some of it is coincidence for us: Jonathan has had a relationship with the Daniel Rion wines for a long time, since he used to work at Moore Brothers way way back in the dark ages, and encountered Rion through them.  The prices have remained reasonable, we have been able to source our favorite bottlings in case quantity, and most of our holdings are patiently aging, unlike some others that we drink young and thus go through fast.  It's a way to access taste of the Cote de Nuits on an ongoing basis.  Don't get me wrong, Rully and Savigny are delightful, but they aren't Vosne or even NSG, and therefore not a replacement.  Even the best orange is a terrible peach!

Over the years, we've explored some other producers that fall into roughly the same category, and have added several to the cellar; but so far haven't found one that checks these same boxes for us.  Rion makes us happy.

Finally, we do have a sizeable chunk of wine from other semi-affordable Burgundy producers, but it takes quite a few bottles to get into the Top 10 given that our top few holdings are alarmingly large.

So, that's how we come to have Rion on the list.  Circling back to the beginning - we like them.  I can't say if you should reevaluate, as your needs and wants are not mine (I'm guessing), but I can honestly say I enjoy the wines greatly and find them to have good QPR.

SK

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.raw: @Sarah_Kirschbaum Thanks for this background on your relationship with Rion. I was intrigued by this as well. I too really enjoy the Rion wines and have been buying them intermittently the last decade or so, as well as back-filling to the early 90s. I'm curious what you think about the Daniel Rion wines pre- and post- Patrice Rion as the winemaker. I believe 2002 was his last vintage making the wines before parting ways with (his brother?) and starting his own winery, labeled either as Domaine Rion or Patrice Rion. I had the pleasure of meeting Patrice back in 2015 and have focused on his wines. I'm wondering if you have a perspective on differences in wine making style between the two different Rion domaines.

I’m curious what you think about the Daniel Rion wines pre- and post- Patrice Rion as the winemaker. I believe 2002 was his last vintage making the wines before parting ways with (his brother?) and starting his own winery, labeled either as Domaine Rion or Patrice Rion. I had the pleasure of meeting Patrice back in 2015 and have focused on his wines. I’m wondering if you have a perspective on differences in wine making style between the two different Rion domaines.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.raw: I don't have as much experience with the Patrice Rion label, but I have found them to be excellent.  Possibly even a little superior (whatever that means) to the early wines, with a little more fine tuned presentation.  No overly made up or anything, just a bit more refined and deft.  I do have a soft spot for the Daniel Rion wines, and have a lot of them from 2002 and before.
posts.cooked: I don’t have as much experience with the Patrice Rion label, but I have found them to be excellent. Possibly even a little superior (whatever that means) to the early wines, with a little more fine tuned presentation. No overly made up or anything, just a bit more refined and deft. I do have a soft spot for the Daniel Rion wines, and have a lot of them from 2002 and before.

posts.name: Warren Taranow
posts.raw:
Significant shifts since I last [posted](https://www.wineberserkers.com/t/top-10-producers-in-your-cellar/74370/46) in this thread 11 1/2 years ago.  Back then it was:

Huët
Rhys
Charvin
Betz
Pégaü
Calon-Ségur
Beaucastel
Félsina
Baumard

Now:

**Krug** (mostly GC, various eme's)
**Louis Roederer** (mostly Cristal)
**Taittinger** (all Comtes Bdb but for a few Comtes Rosé)
**Vilmart**
**Billecart-Salmon** (mostly cuvées Elisabeth, Nicolas, Louis)
**Egly Ouriet**
**Ridge Montebello**
**Lopez de Herredia**
**Dom Perignon**
**Coudert (Clos de la Roilette)**

followed closely by Laurent-Perrier Grand Siecle, Pierre-Yves Colin-Morey, Kelley Fox, Ceritas.
I actually own a lot of Northern Rhone, Piedmont, Red Burg and Bdx, but multiple producers so no one producer shows up at the top.

"Once you start down the Champagne path, forever will it dominate your destiny, consume you it will."

posts.name: Franklin Ro3sn3r
posts.raw:
Just finished a long overdure inventory and can look at this data for the first time since pre-COVID.  Fun exercise.  Top 10 is all domestic (a bit of a surprise).  (edit: the software is autocorrecting my numbering, it shoud show a tie at number 10 and a 3-way ti for 12).

1.   Seavey Vineyard        4.1% (favorite "relatively" QPR domestic, long-term agers)
2.   Rhys                           3.5%. (despite lack of consensus, think these are and will continue to age well)
3.   Paul Hobbs                 3.0%  (wife's fave)
4.   Williams Selyem         2.1%. (so good and pleases everyone!)
5.   Aubert                        2.0%.  (surprised me, doing some science for aging domestic whites)
6.   Vincent                      1.7%.   (thanks, WineBerserkers!)
7.   Peter Michael            1.5%.  (see #5)
8.   Kistler                        1.5%.  (holding more of the Pinots after tasting a '98 Kistler Vineyard that blew my socks off)
9.   Peay Vineyards         1.5%.  (enjoy their wines, but drinking and buying less)
10. Littorai                       1.3%.   (the "ugly duckliing" label, beautiful reds and whites 5++ years out)
10. Outpost                     1.3%.  (only buying the Grenache and Zin after the sale to AXA but do love 'em)
12. DuPuis                      1.0%.  (hoping the best for Wells and, selfishly, that means more winemaking)
12. Château Montrose.   1.0%.  (Ah, finally a French representation!  Hope I live long enough...)
12. La Rata Wines          1.0%.  (have a verticle from the '12 vintage, sorry that story is coming to an end)
posts.cooked:
Just finished a long overdure inventory and can look at this data for the first time since pre-COVID. Fun exercise. Top 10 is all domestic (a bit of a surprise). (edit: the software is autocorrecting my numbering, it shoud show a tie at number 10 and a 3-way ti for 12).

  1. Seavey Vineyard 4.1% (favorite “relatively” QPR domestic, long-term agers)
  2. Rhys 3.5%. (despite lack of consensus, think these are and will continue to age well)
  3. Paul Hobbs 3.0% (wife’s fave)
  4. Williams Selyem 2.1%. (so good and pleases everyone!)
  5. Aubert 2.0%. (surprised me, doing some science for aging domestic whites)
  6. Vincent 1.7%. (thanks, WineBerserkers!)
  7. Peter Michael 1.5%. (see #5)
  8. Kistler 1.5%. (holding more of the Pinots after tasting a '98 Kistler Vineyard that blew my socks off)
  9. Peay Vineyards 1.5%. (enjoy their wines, but drinking and buying less)
  10. Littorai 1.3%. (the “ugly duckliing” label, beautiful reds and whites 5++ years out)
  11. Outpost 1.3%. (only buying the Grenache and Zin after the sale to AXA but do love 'em)
  12. DuPuis 1.0%. (hoping the best for Wells and, selfishly, that means more winemaking)
  13. Château Montrose. 1.0%. (Ah, finally a French representation! Hope I live long enough…)
  14. La Rata Wines 1.0%. (have a verticle from the '12 vintage, sorry that story is coming to an end)

posts.name: John Telford
posts.raw:
**So I took my list and ask ChatGbt the following":**   

*"These are my top producers in my cellar what does this say about my personality and lifestyle"*

**Here was the response:**

### 1. **A Love for Diversity with a Core of Tradition**

* Your cellar spans multiple regions and styles: Bordeaux (both Left and Right Bank), Loire Valley, Beaujolais, and Piedmont. This breadth indicates intellectual curiosity and a desire to experience the full spectrum of what wine offers while staying grounded in classic styles.

### 2. **An Appreciation for Elegance and Restraint**

* Producers like Château Thivin and Coudert (known for Beaujolais) showcase your preference for wines that are pure, expressive, and food-friendly. These wines suggest a sensibility that values refinement over bombast in both wine and life.

### 3. **A Deep Respect for Terroir and Craftsmanship**

* Producers such as Baudry, Raffault, and Vallana emphasize terroir-driven wines with authenticity and character. You’re drawn to craftsmanship that honors nature and heritage, which likely extends to other areas of your life, such as your taste in art, design, or cuisine.

### 4. **A Patient and Reflective Collector**

* Many of these wines, such as Grand-Puy-Lacoste, Clinet, and Malartic-Lagravière, are built to age gracefully, requiring time to reveal their full potential. This indicates you’re someone who enjoys planning ahead and savoring the rewards of patience.

### 5. **A Preference for Both Complexity and Approachability**

* With Château Sociando-Mallet and Lanessan, you balance wines that are layered and intriguing yet accessible. You likely enjoy experiences that are intellectually stimulating but not overly complicated, making you approachable yet insightful in social settings.

### 6. **A Sophisticated Adventurer**

* Including Vallana e Figlio in your cellar hints at an appreciation for lesser-known gems, showing your willingness to venture off the beaten path. This reflects a personality that enjoys discovering hidden treasures in both wine and life.

### 7. **A Thoughtful Host**

* Your selection ranges from the festive and versatile (Beaujolais from Thivin or Coudert) to the rich and indulgent (Clinet or Grand-Puy-Lacoste). This suggests you take pleasure in tailoring experiences for others, perhaps hosting gatherings where wine becomes a centerpiece of shared enjoyment.

posts.name: John Telford
posts.raw:
Finally, I asked it to recommend a producer not on my list.

Answer:

Given your thoughtful collection and its emphasis on tradition, terroir, and craftsmanship, I suggest adding **Hermann J. Wiemer** from the **Finger Lakes region of New York**, specifically their **Dry Riesling** or **Single Vineyard Riesling (e.g., HJW Vineyard)**. Here's why:

### Why Hermann J. Wiemer?

1. **A New Region with Deep Roots**:
  * The Finger Lakes has become a benchmark region for Riesling in the U.S., combining cool climate elegance with Old World winemaking principles. Wiemer is a pioneer, blending Germanic tradition with American innovation.
2. **Terroir-Driven Precision**:
  * Wiemer's Rieslings showcase the Finger Lakes’ slate-rich soils and cool climate, producing wines of high acidity, precision, and age-worthiness. This would complement your cellar's focus on terroir-driven wines like Baudry or Raffault.
3. **Versatility and Pairing**:
  * Riesling's versatility—whether dry or with slight residual sweetness—offers a dynamic pairing partner for diverse cuisines, from seafood to spicy dishes, enriching your collection's food-friendly options.
4. **Unique Yet Classical**:
  * Wiemer’s Rieslings combine a sense of discovery (a less-represented American region) with classical refinement, fitting perfectly into your existing mix of traditional yet adventurous selections.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.raw:
[quote="JBrochu, post:843, topic:74370, full:true"]

Amiot et Fils

[/quote]

Interesting inclusion.  I have visited there twice in recent years (including summer 2023), and like their wines very much.  In fact, I liked their 2021s (which is what we tasted in 2023) better than prior vintages.  Not sure whether I just really like the vintage (probably a likely factor), whether it is the addition of Leon (son of Jean-Louis and Chantal) now being at the winery or what but the 2021s seem really good.  

Now I hope someday to have some of their wines when they actually are mature.

posts.name: Bill Schneller
posts.raw:
1. Hofgut Falkenstein
2. Cameron
3. Arterberry Maresh
4. Shiba Wichern
5. Goodfellow
6. Evesham Wood
7. JC Somers
8. Vincent
9. Crowley
10. (Tie) Belle Pente and Coudert

Falkenstein used to be number 2 or 3 but I went pretty heavy on 2023 (mostly because I was offered a much larger and broader allocation than in previous years).  Cameron was number one for as long as I can remember until Falkenstein edged them out. There are a couple of producers right below this, like Violin, Tim Malone, and Granville who either used to be top ten or are moving in that direction.  I'll be curious to see what it is in a year or two.

posts.name: Steve Costigan
posts.raw: The top wines/producers in my cellar haven’t changed much but I just figured out that 41% of the wines in my cellar are present as just one bottle and 55% of the wines are present as just one or two bottles. I was curious on that because I’ve always tended towards a wider range than going deep on any single wine. I’ve rarely purchased more than 3 or 4 bottles of a single wine and the ones that have the largest current inventory are so by virtue of collecting them over many vintages.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.raw:
Sarah, I was wondering, would your top 10 cuvées be the same as your top 10 producers?

For example, I assume your Taittinger is close to 100% Comtes whereas others might be across the producer's range?

I find that in Burgundy and Barolo/Barbaresco, I tend to buy pretty broadly over the entire producer's range. However, in Bordeaux (almost no 2nd+ wines), Champagne or Northern Rhône, I cherry-pick the cuvées I purchase from a given producer.

I don't know why this hit me reading your post, but it did 😆.
posts.cooked:

I don’t know why this hit me reading your post, but it did .

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.raw:
Hi Phil - Interesting question.  It varies, but more often than not we have a variety of cuvees (take note those who think we don't like variety! ;-) ) Here's a rough idea below.

[quote="Sarah_Kirschbaum, post:889, topic:74370"]

Keller - many different bottlings
Emrich-Schönleber - many different bottlings
Schäfer-Fröhlich - many different bottlings

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.raw:
[quote="Phil_T_r_o_t_t_e_r, post:890, topic:74370, full:true"]
Sarah, I was wondering, would your top 10 cuvées be the same as your top 10 producers?

For example, I assume your Taittinger is close to 100% Comtes whereas others might be across the producer's range?

I find that in Burgundy and Barolo/Barbaresco, I tend to buy pretty broadly over the entire producer's range. However, in Bordeaux (almost no 2nd+ wines), Champagne or Northern Rhône, I cherry-pick the cuvées I purchase from a given producer.

I don't know why this hit me reading your post, but it did 😆.
[/quote]

In Bordeaux, what do you consider to be a producer?  In Burgundy or Germany, for example, if a producer buys a new plot of land, the name of the producer will be on the label and so will the name of the vineyard (new or old).  But, what about the same in Bordeaux.  For example, Claire Villars-Lurton owns both Chateau Ferriere and Chateau Haut Bages Liberal.  Her husband owns Chateau Durfort Vivens. [G&C Lurton | Bordeaux Wine Production | The Family](https://www.gc-lurton-estates.com/en/family.html)    One winery, two or three?  And, if you start with the Lurtons, not sure where this ends.
posts.cooked:
Phil_T_r_o_t_t_e_r:

Sarah, I was wondering, would your top 10 cuvées be the same as your top 10 producers?

For example, I assume your Taittinger is close to 100% Comtes whereas others might be across the producer’s range?

I find that in Burgundy and Barolo/Barbaresco, I tend to buy pretty broadly over the entire producer’s range. However, in Bordeaux (almost no 2nd+ wines), Champagne or Northern Rhône, I cherry-pick the cuvées I purchase from a given producer.

I don’t know why this hit me reading your post, but it did .

In Bordeaux, what do you consider to be a producer? In Burgundy or Germany, for example, if a producer buys a new plot of land, the name of the producer will be on the label and so will the name of the vineyard (new or old). But, what about the same in Bordeaux. For example, Claire Villars-Lurton owns both Chateau Ferriere and Chateau Haut Bages Liberal. Her husband owns Chateau Durfort Vivens. G&C Lurton | Bordeaux Wine Production | The Family One winery, two or three? And, if you start with the Lurtons, not sure where this ends.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.raw: To be honest, I don't have an answer to my question.  I certainly think when a husband and wife own wineries separately, they are two separate estates - like PYCM and his wife Caroline Morey.  But, then I have a hard time thinking of say Comte Lafon's wines in Meursault and those in Macon as two separate wineries and so why would two Bordeaux estates owned by one producer be two different properties?  But, then what about a Hudelot-Noellat and Charles Van Canneyt.  Charles runs both properties, although I think other family members own part of Hudelot-Noellat.  So, I guess I come down to we each answer the question as we best see fit and it is what it is.  This is only wine after all.

So, I guess I come down to we each answer the question as we best see fit and it is what it is. This is only wine after all.

posts.name: Cameron Case
posts.raw:
Hello everyone, I'm new in town and have only been tasting and studying wine for about a half decade, so my holdings are comparatively meager at this stage. First post and figured this would be a decent introduction to the community here. 

1. Domaine Arlaud 
First full case of wine I ever purchased was their '21 "Roncevie" Bourgogne Rouge made from 60 yo declassified Gevrey vines. A eureka moment in my budding wine journey and still among the best Bourgogne AOC I have yet been able to taste. At 32 bucks a bottle I really had no choice in the matter.

2. Patricia Green Cellars
Being this late to the game means most Burgundy remains tragically too expensive for me to explore, so I appreciate the commitment to place that these wines have. A more affordable way to explore single vineyard expressions that taught me a lot about terroir early on in my pinot studies.

3. Bernard Baudry
My ideal everyday "bistro" wines. I cook french a good deal and love having these around. Affordable, available, but ageworthy and serious with some like Les Granges being great on release. There is a certain comfort to their rusticity, like a well worn sweater on an autumn day.

4. Jean Foillard
One of my very first loves, the '16 Corcelette being my first experience with anything resembling low intervention winemaking I can recall. Will always remember the fragrance of violets and lilac wafting from the glass that late summer evening. Another eureka moment. Handful of cuvees in the cellar silently ripening.

5. Montepeloso
Bit of an outlier here, I picked up a mixed lot of back vintages at a heavily reduced price out of curiosity after skimming some tech sheets. At the time I was introducing myself to Tuscany and these felt idiosyncratic and worthy of exploration. Like the pierced and tattooed sibling to a Super Tuscan. Savory, animale, and brooding. I like that I have several of these mysteries in my closet and can visit them over the coming years. 

6. Mas Des Capitelles
This is on the strength of one wine on its own, and that wine is their 2020 Faugeres Vieilles Vignes. I am convinced this is the greatest QPR wine on the planet. Massive fruit, spicy, deep and moody with heavenly lift and grace. I almost don't even want to share this fact because I want every bottle to myself. 20 Bucks. I bought every bottle I could find. 

7. Felsina
I love the elegance of their style. I have come to prefer Sangiovese in its more delicate and slightly austere expression and these to me are spot on. The Rancia with its spices and herbs is one of my favorite wines, and one I hope to try with significant age someday. 

8. JK Carriere
Very early in my wine journey, way back in 2021 :wink: the 2014 Vespidae was my first Willamette pinot. Sort of the genesis for my Oregon fascination. Admittedly there is a bit of nostalgia here but I remember being struck by how vivid this wine felt compared to whatever domestic plonk I had tasted up to that point. Glad to have a few vintages, like an old friend.

9. Salvo Foti-I Vigneri
Much like the Montepeloso, sort of a shot in the dark on this one. Last year was my Etna year, and my local merchant had taken notice. A few of these were placed in my hands accompanied by reverent and hushed tones of praise from my trusted shopkeep. The whites are luminous and shimmering, the reds are explosive and delicate in equal measure. Wish I had more.

10. Chateau Thivin
Coming to terms with the Cote D'or being largely out of reach, I have endeavored to fill that void with its southern neighbor. I love this structured and vinous style of Gamay and Thivin does it so well. Thankful to have a smattering of various bottles. Great with some age.

That about does it. My whole collection comes in at a whopping 123 bottles. Thankfully I have my whole life to spend my hard earned cash on fermented grapes, so check back in 2028 as by then I may even have a 5th growth or two.... Cheers.
posts.cooked:
Hello everyone, I’m new in town and have only been tasting and studying wine for about a half decade, so my holdings are comparatively meager at this stage. First post and figured this would be a decent introduction to the community here.

  1. Domaine Arlaud

A eureka moment in my budding wine journey and still among the best Bourgogne AOC I have yet been able to taste. At 32 bucks a bottle I really had no choice in the matter.

  2. Patricia Green Cellars
    Being this late to the game means most Burgundy remains tragically too expensive for me to explore, so I appreciate the commitment to place that these wines have. A more affordable way to explore single vineyard expressions that taught me a lot about terroir early on in my pinot studies.

  3. Bernard Baudry
    My ideal everyday “bistro” wines. I cook french a good deal and love having these around. Affordable, available, but ageworthy and serious with some like Les Granges being great on release. There is a certain comfort to their rusticity, like a well worn sweater on an autumn day.

  4. Jean Foillard
    One of my very first loves, the '16 Corcelette being my first experience with anything resembling low intervention winemaking I can recall. Will always remember the fragrance of violets and lilac wafting from the glass that late summer evening. Another eureka moment. Handful of cuvees in the cellar silently ripening.

  5. Montepeloso
    Bit of an outlier here, I picked up a mixed lot of back vintages at a heavily reduced price out of curiosity after skimming some tech sheets. At the time I was introducing myself to Tuscany and these felt idiosyncratic and worthy of exploration. Like the pierced and tattooed sibling to a Super Tuscan. Savory, animale, and brooding. I like that I have several of these mysteries in my closet and can visit them over the coming years.

  6. Mas Des Capitelles
    This is on the strength of one wine on its own, and that wine is their 2020 Faugeres Vieilles Vignes. I am convinced this is the greatest QPR wine on the planet. Massive fruit, spicy, deep and moody with heavenly lift and grace. I almost don’t even want to share this fact because I want every bottle to myself. 20 Bucks. I bought every bottle I could find.

  7. Felsina
    I love the elegance of their style. I have come to prefer Sangiovese in its more delicate and slightly austere expression and these to me are spot on. The Rancia with its spices and herbs is one of my favorite wines, and one I hope to try with significant age someday.

  8. JK Carriere
    Very early in my wine journey, way back in 2021 the 2014 Vespidae was my first Willamette pinot. Sort of the genesis for my Oregon fascination. Admittedly there is a bit of nostalgia here but I remember being struck by how vivid this wine felt compared to whatever domestic plonk I had tasted up to that point. Glad to have a few vintages, like an old friend.

  9. Salvo Foti-I Vigneri
    Much like the Montepeloso, sort of a shot in the dark on this one. Last year was my Etna year, and my local merchant had taken notice. A few of these were placed in my hands accompanied by reverent and hushed tones of praise from my trusted shopkeep. The whites are luminous and shimmering, the reds are explosive and delicate in equal measure. Wish I had more.

  10. Chateau Thivin
    Coming to terms with the Cote D’or being largely out of reach, I have endeavored to fill that void with its southern neighbor. I love this structured and vinous style of Gamay and Thivin does it so well. Thankful to have a smattering of various bottles. Great with some age.

That about does it. My whole collection comes in at a whopping 123 bottles. Thankfully I have my whole life to spend my hard earned cash on fermented grapes, so check back in 2028 as by then I may even have a 5th growth or two… Cheers.
