posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.raw:
Welcome Cam!

If Patty Green is your go-to OR Pinot, you are in good hands for sure.  

Don’t give up on Burgundy yet - there are definitely some affordable truffles to be found in the rich soil of the Côte d'Or if you explore “lesser” appellations like Fixin, Marsannay, and Auxey to name a few. 

For more see: https://www.wineberserkers.com/t/indroduction-to-burgundy/323455/9

Count me as a particular fan of Cyril Audoin and Gilles Lafouge for delicious Burgundy value. 

@Robert_Panzer is an importer with a palate I trust who reliably finds talented up-and-comers like Audoin and Lafouge. If you appreciate Riesling and Champagne (and you should :cheers:) sample Max Ferd. Richter’s WS Spatlese and Ployez-Jaquemart bubbly.

Trusted negociant producers also offer a way into Burgundy: Try Joseph Drouhin’s Clos de Mouches or step up for the Chambolle 1er blend ($100+). Jadot’s Beaune Ursules is a reliable favorite. [
Bouchard recently shed its’  negociant holdings](https://www.wineberserkers.com/t/bouchard-pere-fils-withdraws-from-en-primeur-and-exits-the-negociant-business/333257/3), but seek out their 16 le Corton for an easy-drinking taste of grand cru value. Frédéric Weber just has a way with wines that I like. 

Faiveley may well offer values but I have less experience with them. Their 17 Mercurey clos Rochette blanc was my white wine wake-up call that Chardonnay was a thing I liked.

Count me as a particular fan of Cyril Audoin and Gilles Lafouge for delicious Burgundy value.

Trusted negociant producers also offer a way into Burgundy: Try Joseph Drouhin’s Clos de Mouches or step up for the Chambolle 1er blend ($100+). Jadot’s Beaune Ursules is a reliable favorite.
Bouchard recently shed its’ negociant holdings, but seek out their 16 le Corton for an easy-drinking taste of grand cru value. Frédéric Weber just has a way with wines that I like.

posts.name: Cameron Case
posts.raw: Hi Steve, thanks for the insight! A cursory scroll suggests I have had the good fortune to sample several producers scattered throughout the @Robert_Panzer portfolio, and it now seems I will be forging a closer bond with messieurs Audoin and Lafouge. I do happen to be a great admirer of the more "humble" villages of Burgundy, in fact I likely drink more such wines than any other the world over, and my cynicism is frequently checked by the abundance of quality I encounter (most recently by a Marsannay from Domaine du Vieux College, unbelievable depth and interest for €25). Make no mistake, my pursuit is ceaseless. I will fill this absurd bingo card of vineyards before my time is up. I may grumble only because I am haunted by the knowledge that one could snag some Nuits-Saint-Georges for less than a plane ticket only a handful of years ago. :crazy_face:

posts.name: G av_1n 40r es1
posts.raw:
Donnhoff
Beta
Walter Scott
Tempier
Pepiere
Cotat
Goodfellow
Jamet
Martin Mullen
Vieux Telegraphe

*on the line: Jean-Louis Dutraive (Domaine de la Grand’Cour), Chapoutier, and Pegau*

Last year's predictions were mostly accurate. 
Jamet stayed level. Donnhoff gained as I backfilled my vertical. Tempier did depend on backfilling (got a couple lucky auction pickups), but consumption also slowed. WS is a great value and their list was one of the only I kept this year. Dutraive did get overtaken due to price elevation relative to my initial buy in. Same with Gonon. Cotat did hang on for longer (some lucky breaks in secondary). Scholium and No girls fell off. Scholium I drank down. No Girls should never have been there. Goodfellow is actually much higher, but that is due to 4 extra cases for an event next week.

**What came up?** Beta. I'd worried the last CA cabs I like were made in =< '94. This is the only current release I'm buying in Napa. I made an effort to get more old Pepiere. Was tremendously impressed with Martin Mullen during my travels this year and so grabbed a bushel to think with. Vieux Telegraphe, Chapoutier, and Pegau --  Lady Luck + zero'ing in on some vintage preferences. 

**Predictions forwards.**  The decline curve is still pretty step for this top 10 (exploring / region level buying is still my primary enjoyment) and I expect continued movement. 
Jamet stays. Feel increasingly confident this is my home in the NR. Donnhoff increases as I continue to build the vertical. Tempier, once again, depends on backfilling, but tough to think my luck finding pre-2000 bottles at good pricing will continue. Pepiere and Cotat also backfill dependent with an easier age hurdle. Earlier this year, I thought that the combination of having many and my impression of increased reductive notes with the wines would have me pause on Walter Scott, but during my last visit the reductive notes seemed to be calmer? Maybe some in person magic. Have a decision to make here. Beta will increase as I max my allocation. Mullen - I need to figure out what type of Alien this is. Big tasting followed by double down or backing off.

VT / Jean-Louis Dutraive (Domaine de la Grand’Cour) / Chapoutier / Pegau - Like them all, but coin flip, probably down. A sparkling or sherry could very well join the top 10, as the quantity is there, just not concentrated on one producer.

Biggest change this year is a lot of: 
Bernaudeau +14 bottles
Labet +16 bottles

Biggest holding is still these names as I know they'll drink amazing in +10 years.
Labet
Cavarodes
Foillard
Cailloux du Paradis
De Moor
Naudin Ferrand
Lino Maga

L'Anglore (Can find in Paris and Copenhagen at most restaurants for a reasonable price)

Pedres Blanques (as with RdJ unreasonable price increase)

posts.name: Mark_JC
posts.raw:
[quote="Max_S1, post:972, topic:74370, full:true"]
How is white rock? Saw their nase bottling on the shelf at my local store and the tag sounded good…
[/quote]

I am a big fan. You can probably tell from my percentages that it was my first wine membership. :grinning: 

I love White Rock’s reds and whites equally, and they are great even without a long decant. When I want to pop and pour, White Rock is my go-to while other bottles need more time (e.g., KE). The young wines and the older ones both shine. 

I have several in my cellar, and I am happy to share if you ever want to try a bottle.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.raw:
Nice. I quite like Yvon Clerget - first ran into the wines at the ‘15 or ‘16 La Paulee and bought a few vintages. Especially fine Caillerets. I’ve never bought the Clos Vougeot though. Have you tried much of that one?

I stumbled across a few bottles of the 1999 Caillerets, made by the father (grandfather?) of the current young wine maker. Another excellent exemplar of that vintage.

posts.name: John Maguire
posts.raw:
Only two data points for me on Clerget, but posted here fwiw: 2019 Carelle sous la Chappelle (tasted 1/22) and 2018 Verseuil (tasted 3/23).  Both very tasty. The Verseuil offered more Volnay silk to my taste. The Carelle was still forcefully primary. The 2018 Verseuil seemed about to go into a grumpy phase. 
These two data points offered significantly different experiences, although both were firmly Burgundian, and I definitely enjoyed both. YMMV

posts.name: Robert Lipman
posts.raw: I don’t make too many good ones lol. I convinced my significant other to make a detour from Paris to actually visit Champagne for my 30th birthday. We ended up staying at the Selosse estate and we ordered a bottle of the Initial. I’ll never forget her reaction haha. We basically decided right then that this had to be ‘our’ wine, for life’s biggest special occasions. I immediately started looking for whatever I could get my hands on. I only have a couple of bottles but we’re gonna make them count!

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
[quote="Mikael_OB, post:781, topic:74370, full:true"]
[quote="Mikael_OB, post:526, topic:74370, full:true"]
[quote="Mikael_OB, post:464, topic:74370, full:true"]
Interesting to see, especially the development over time.

My top 10 (out of 280):

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Henrik Möbitz
5. Weiser-Künstler
6. Stein
7 Daniel Bouland
8. Ca' del Baio
9. La Ca'Növa
10. Baudry/Dettori

Only a few bottles apart from 10 and 20 with more Italian (Barolo mainly) producers.

A bit surprised to see how may Germany entries in top 10, knew E&M as well as Möbitz would be quite high up. 6-9 are wines more recently purchased, the two Barbaresco producers plenty of 2016 bottles (birth year).
[/quote]

One of the main change is that white wine producers have climbed up a few spots. Intentional change, as a) didn't have enough, to high red wine ratio in general b) my daughters birth year is 2020 and that give me even more motivation to fill up on white wines (many red have not yet been released yet like Barolo/Barbaresco). 

Sandri and Burlotto has worked their way to the top 10 list as well, and that's related to "b" to some extent as I have bought quite a few of the 2020 LNs, but also 2016s Sandri Barolos (my son's birth year). For me the Burlotto is more near term consumption (perhaps medium and up to around 10 years I can imagine), while the Sandri LN have the stuffing for aging - I have found them to many times be better/more real with soul then many Barolos. 

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Stein
5. Weiser-Künstler
6. Henrik Möbitz 
7. Elio Sandri
8. Comm. G.B. Burlotto
9. Hofgut Falkenstein 
10. Weingut Keller

A few bottles bottles behind 10th are the wines that got pushed out of the list.
[/quote]

About a year and a half later and there are some movement, mainly driven by focusing on adding white wines, and working on the cellar reduction plan has forced me to be even more focused in purchases. 

The main changes are that Falkenstein, Stein and Keller has kept moving on up. 
Ca Nova was just outside top 10 last time, a few bottles of 2020 (birth year wines) added and they're back in. 

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Falkenstein
5. Stein
6. Keller
7. Weiser-Künstler
8. La Ca' Növa
9. Burlotto
10.  Henrik Möbitz / Sandri
[/quote]

Only minor movements in the top 10: 

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Stein
5. Falkenstein
6. Keller
7. Burlotto
8. La Ca Nova
9. Henrik Möbitz 
10. Weiser-Künstler / Sandri

Wines from 288 different wineries (47 with only 1 bottle)

With the inventory reduction ambition, the main movement are hopefully a result of consumption in 2026, with modest purchase of favorites and perhaps a few minor explorations here and there.

  10. Weingut Keller

A few bottles bottles behind 10th are the wines that got pushed out of the list.

About a year and a half later and there are some movement, mainly driven by focusing on adding white wines, and working on the cellar reduction plan has forced me to be even more focused in purchases.

The main changes are that Falkenstein, Stein and Keller has kept moving on up.
Ca Nova was just outside top 10 last time, a few bottles of 2020 (birth year wines) added and they’re back in.

  1. Ronchi di Cialla
  2. Milos
  3. Enderle & Moll
  4. Falkenstein
  5. Stein
  6. Keller
  7. Weiser-Künstler
  8. La Ca’ Növa
  9. Burlotto
  10. Henrik Möbitz / Sandri

Only minor movements in the top 10:

  1. Ronchi di Cialla
  2. Milos
  3. Enderle & Moll
  4. Stein
  5. Falkenstein
  6. Keller
  7. Burlotto
  8. La Ca Nova
  9. Henrik Möbitz
  10. Weiser-Künstler / Sandri

Wines from 288 different wineries (47 with only 1 bottle)

With the inventory reduction ambition, the main movement are hopefully a result of consumption in 2026, with modest purchase of favorites and perhaps a few minor explorations here and there.

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.raw:
[quote="Wil_Raley, post:880, topic:74370"]
A little shuffling going on in the last year.

Fourrier - 15.4%
Raveneau - 12.4%
Dauvissat - 9.8%
Paul Pillot - 7.4%
Antica Terra - 7.1%
Kelley Fox - 4.8%
Denis Mortet - 4%
Mugneret-Gibourg - 3.8%
Walter Scott 3.6%
Comtes Lafon tied w/ Roumier at 3.1%

[/quote]

A lot of movement this year. My buying is becoming more focused as I continue to dial in my palate. I am not purchasing as much Fourrier as I once was (no longer backfilling), but still love the wines, and will continue to purchase new vintages. I anticipate Morgen Long will clime in to the #1 spot as Seth’s wines strike the perfect balance for my personal QPR - they sit on the same plain as PYCM from a quality perspective IMO. Speaking of PYCM - this is a gap in my cellar that I intend to fill. The big five for me heading into 2026 are Paul Pillot, Raveneau, Dauvissat, PYCM and Mugneret Gibourg.

 1. **Domaine Fourrier** – (14.8%)
 2. **François Raveneau** – (10.8%)
 3. **Morgen Long** – (9.2%)

 9. **Denis Mortet** – (3.1%)
10. **Latour-Giraud** – (3.1%)
11. **Lamy-Caillat**  – (3.1%)

posts.name: Tony Mulkey
posts.raw:
Still a baby collector and diversifying my interests, but I have been primarily focused on prominent California producers building verticals and other fun pairings.

I live near Berns and some of my wine interest was seeded through older vintage larks from the cellar. Recently collecting some older bottles including a bottle older than anything I have tried yet - 1949 4th growth for MIL upcoming bday paired with 3 additional modern vintages.

Recently seeking out a lot more Washington and northern Rhône producers for some variety. Mixed in with sprinklings of some Tuscans and Rioja. Plus some random Australian Syrah, sav blanc (want more Sancerre), lots of Sauternes, and building more Riesling and Bordeaux. Used to be drawn to burgundy but have fallen away in recent years, and I do need more Barolo and Barbaresco. So many options!

Ridge Vineyards  
Spring Mountain Vineyard 
Mount Eden Vineyards
Kapcsándy Family Winery
Meteor Vineyard
Stag's Leap Wine Cellars
Château Mouton Rothschild
Château d'Yquem
Château Latour
Shafer Vineyards
